generation.name: Seed Content Provider
generation.nextStep: Keep owned MVP content in seed form while curriculum coverage and editorial gates are still stabilising.

generation.name: Future Editorial Pipeline
generation.nextStep: Connect reviewed content generation to CMS tooling without bypassing source attribution or fact-check gates.

supply.name: Content Catalog Module
supply.nextStep: Remain the single source of truth for structured student content across website and future mobile clients.

supply.name: CMS Provider Boundary
supply.nextStep: Attach real CMS adapters only when they respect the existing review and verification workflow.

updates.name: Editorial Audit
updates.nextStep: Use blocked-topic decisions and fact-check counts to control what reaches students.

updates.name: Past Paper Provider Plan
updates.nextStep: Feed future exam paper updates through provider adapters rather than hard-coding them into page routes.

contentOperations.visibilityRule: Only reviewed, source-attributed, and fact-check-verified content can become student visible.

subjects.name: GCSE Mathematics
subjects.description: Core number, algebra, geometry, ratio, and statistics revision.
subjects.endOfYearExamContext: Built for Year 10 students preparing for end-of-year internal exams that already begin to use GCSE command words, timings, and mark-style expectations.
subjects.gcsePreparationGoal: Move students from Year 10 end-of-year confidence into stronger GCSE paper readiness by using progression questions and exam-style routines early.
subjects.boardCoverageNote: Maths coverage is shaped around common AQA, Edexcel, and OCR Year 10 and Year 11 topic sequencing, with room for board-specific paper variants.

subjects.name: GCSE English Language
subjects.description: Reading, inference, language analysis, structure, and writing craft.
subjects.endOfYearExamContext: Built for Year 10 students who need end-of-year English assessments to feel like a bridge into GCSE extract work, inference, and analytical writing.
subjects.gcsePreparationGoal: Prepare students for GCSE English Language expectations by introducing the same habits of evidence selection, analysis, and timed response structure before Year 11.
subjects.boardCoverageNote: English coverage follows shared Year 10 and Year 11 reading-analysis expectations across Edexcel, AQA, and Eduqas before more specific paper wording is layered in.

subjects.name: GCSE Combined Science
subjects.description: Biology, chemistry, and physics concepts through exam-ready checkpoints.
subjects.endOfYearExamContext: Built for Year 10 end-of-year science exams where students are expected to answer increasingly GCSE-style explanation and practical-method questions.
subjects.gcsePreparationGoal: Use Year 10 science revision and progression checks to make the jump into GCSE-style application and required-practical questions feel familiar.
subjects.boardCoverageNote: Science coverage is organised around Year 10 foundations and Year 11 application work common to AQA, Edexcel, and OCR combined science courses.

subjects.name: iGCSE Mathematics
subjects.description: iGCSE maths coverage for students bridging from advanced Year 10 work into international GCSE paper styles.
subjects.endOfYearExamContext: Built for advanced Year 10 students whose end-of-year work is already beginning to resemble iGCSE algebra and graph interpretation questions.
subjects.gcsePreparationGoal: Bridge strong Year 10 maths students into iGCSE command words, graph reasoning, and paper expectations before full Year 11 exam pressure.
subjects.boardCoverageNote: iGCSE maths coverage begins with shared Cambridge and Edexcel International GCSE foundations, then expands into board-specific paper variants.

topics.name: Algebra
topics.summary: Expressions, equations, rearranging formulae, and sequences.

studentContext.endOfYearExamUse: Use this topic for Year 10 end-of-year algebra papers that start to mirror GCSE working and method-mark expectations.
studentContext.gcsePreparationBridge: These questions build fluency first, then bridge students into the style and precision expected in full GCSE algebra items.

curriculumCoverage.year10CoverageNote: In Year 10 this topic supports expansion, simplification, and early equation fluency used in end-of-year papers.
curriculumCoverage.year11CoverageNote: In Year 11 the same topic feeds directly into higher-mark GCSE algebra manipulation and problem solving.

visualSupport.generatedImagePrompt: A clean study visual for Year 10 algebra revision showing brackets expanding into like terms, equation steps, and sequence patterns with GCSE-style annotations.
visualSupport.altText: Year 10 algebra progression visual showing worked algebra steps and GCSE-style method cues.

sections.title: Explain Simply
sections.body: Algebra is a way to describe missing numbers and relationships using symbols.

sections.title: Worked Examples
sections.body: Expand brackets step by step, then combine like terms carefully.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
sections.body: Dropping minus signs and mixing unlike terms are the fastest ways to lose marks.

sections.title: Exam Technique
sections.body: Show one clean line of working for every transformation so follow-through marks stay available.

quiz.prompt: Which expression is equivalent to 5(x + 2) - x?

topics.name: Ratio
topics.summary: Scale factors, proportion, rates, and direct comparison.

studentContext.endOfYearExamUse: Use this topic in Year 10 end-of-year maths papers where ratio starts to appear in map-scale, recipe, and multi-step GCSE-style contexts.
studentContext.gcsePreparationBridge: Students move from straightforward ratio methods into more layered GCSE-style reasoning and unit-control questions.

curriculumCoverage.year10CoverageNote: In Year 10 this topic supports proportional reasoning and scale work commonly introduced before mock-paper difficulty.
curriculumCoverage.year11CoverageNote: In Year 11 the same topic supports more complex GCSE multi-step ratio, rates, and scale interpretation.

visualSupport.generatedImagePrompt: A Year 10 ratio study visual with bar-model parts, map scale conversions, recipe scaling, and GCSE-style unit labels.
visualSupport.altText: Year 10 ratio progression visual showing parts, scale conversions, and GCSE-style ratio reasoning.

sections.title: Explain Simply
sections.body: Ratio compares parts and helps you scale one quantity against another accurately.

sections.title: Worked Examples
sections.body: Write the total number of parts first, then divide the full amount before multiplying back up.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
sections.body: Students often reverse the ratio order or forget to simplify before comparing.

sections.title: Exam Technique
sections.body: Label units carefully, especially when ratio appears inside a map scale or recipe question.

quiz.prompt: A ratio is 2:3. If the total is 20, what is the larger part?

sourceAttribution.checkedAgainst: GCSE ratio and proportion topic outline

topics.name: Language Analysis
topics.summary: Selecting quotations and explaining word-level effects clearly.

studentContext.endOfYearExamUse: Use this topic for Year 10 end-of-year English exams that begin to expect GCSE-style extract analysis and quotation selection.
studentContext.gcsePreparationBridge: Students build the habit of precise quotation choice and analytical explanation before full GCSE language analysis questions become high stakes.

curriculumCoverage.year10CoverageNote: In Year 10 this topic supports extract analysis habits and evidence selection for end-of-year English assessments.
curriculumCoverage.year11CoverageNote: In Year 11 it feeds directly into GCSE language-analysis paragraphs under timed conditions.

visualSupport.generatedImagePrompt: A Year 10 English Language analysis visual showing quotation selection, method spotting, and effect explanation with GCSE-style paragraph cues.
visualSupport.altText: Year 10 English language analysis progression visual showing quotation, method, and effect structure.

sections.title: Explain Simply
sections.body: Language analysis is about what the writer chose and why it affects the reader.

sections.title: Worked Examples
sections.body: Start with a short quotation, identify a method, then explain its effect in context.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
sections.body: Retelling the extract instead of analysing the wording keeps answers stuck at low marks.

sections.title: Exam Technique
sections.body: Use precise verbs like suggests, emphasises, and highlights rather than vague comments.

quiz.prompt: What is the strongest opening move in a language analysis paragraph?

options.label: B
options.text: Choose a short quotation and identify the writer's method

options.label: D
options.text: List every adjective in the extract

sourceAttribution.checkedAgainst: GCSE English Language analysis topic outline

topics.name: Structure
topics.summary: Tracking viewpoint, shifts in focus, and structural impact.

studentContext.endOfYearExamUse: Use this topic in Year 10 end-of-year English papers where students must explain how a text shifts focus in ways that resemble GCSE structure questions.
studentContext.gcsePreparationBridge: Students practise tracking movement and focus now so GCSE structure answers feel like a continuation rather than a brand-new demand.

curriculumCoverage.year10CoverageNote: In Year 10 this topic helps students track shifts in focus and sequence before higher-pressure papers.
curriculumCoverage.year11CoverageNote: In Year 11 the same structure awareness supports clearer GCSE responses on how texts are organised.

visualSupport.generatedImagePrompt: A Year 10 English structure visual showing text focus shifting from opening to ending, with arrows and GCSE-style structure verbs.
visualSupport.altText: Year 10 English structure progression visual showing how focus shifts across a text.

sections.title: Explain Simply
sections.body: Structure is about how the writer organises attention, not just what the text says.

sections.title: Worked Examples
sections.body: Track where the focus begins, what shifts in the middle, and what the ending leaves behind.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
sections.body: Listing techniques without linking them to the reader's experience keeps answers vague.

sections.title: Exam Technique
sections.body: Use movement language like zooms in, shifts, narrows, and returns to show structural control.

quiz.prompt: What should you track first in a structure answer?

options.label: B
options.text: How the focus of the text moves

options.label: D
options.text: The exact number of paragraphs only

sourceAttribution.checkedAgainst: GCSE English Language structure topic outline

studentContext.endOfYearExamUse: Use this topic in Year 10 science exams that begin to test GCSE-style explanation, prediction, and practical-knowledge responses.
studentContext.gcsePreparationBridge: Students use Year 10 chemistry revision to step into the command words and application style they will meet in GCSE Combined Science.

curriculumCoverage.year10CoverageNote: In Year 10 this topic supports core reactivity and electrolysis knowledge before heavier exam application.
curriculumCoverage.year11CoverageNote: In Year 11 it feeds into required-practical thinking and longer GCSE chemistry explanations.

visualSupport.generatedImagePrompt: A Year 10 chemistry revision visual showing reactivity series, displacement reactions, and electrolysis cues with GCSE-style annotation.
visualSupport.altText: Year 10 chemical changes progression visual showing reactivity and electrolysis links.

sections.title: Explain Simply
sections.body: Chemical changes create new substances, often through reactions that transfer energy.

sections.title: Worked Examples
sections.body: Compare metals using reactivity rules before predicting what will happen in a displacement reaction.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
sections.body: Mixing up ions, atoms, and molecules can scramble electrolysis answers quickly.

sections.title: Exam Technique
sections.body: Name the products clearly and use the reactivity series to justify the answer, not just guess it.

quiz.prompt: What helps you decide if a displacement reaction will happen?

sourceAttribution.checkedAgainst: GCSE chemistry chemical changes topic outline

topics.name: Energy
topics.summary: Stores, transfers, efficiency, and required practical links.

studentContext.endOfYearExamUse: This topic is planned for Year 10 end-of-year and early GCSE-preparation science papers but is still blocked pending verification.
studentContext.gcsePreparationBridge: It is intended to bridge Year 10 energy models into GCSE-style practical and efficiency questions once fact-checking is complete.

curriculumCoverage.year10CoverageNote: In Year 10 this topic supports foundational energy stores and transfer ideas that appear in end-of-year science papers.
curriculumCoverage.year11CoverageNote: In Year 11 it should support more formal GCSE efficiency, practical, and energy-transfer application once verification is complete.

visualSupport.generatedImagePrompt: A planned Year 10 energy revision visual showing energy stores, transfer arrows, and efficiency calculations with GCSE-style labels.
visualSupport.altText: Planned Year 10 energy progression visual showing stores, transfers, and efficiency.

sections.title: Explain Simply
sections.body: Energy moves between stores and pathways, even though it cannot be created or destroyed.

sections.title: Worked Examples
sections.body: Start by naming the initial store, then explain the transfer pathway and the final useful output.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
sections.body: Mixing up transfer pathways with stores is one of the most common mark-dropping errors.

sections.title: Exam Technique
sections.body: In efficiency questions, keep the calculation tidy and always include the final unit or percentage.

quiz.prompt: In an efficiency question, what should you compare?

sourceAttribution.checkedAgainst: Pending GCSE physics energy topic check

topics.name: Linear Graphs
topics.summary: Gradient, intercepts, and graph interpretation for advanced Year 10 and iGCSE preparation.

studentContext.endOfYearExamUse: Use this topic for advanced Year 10 maths students whose end-of-year papers already include graph interpretation and equation links in iGCSE style.
studentContext.gcsePreparationBridge: This topic helps students move from classroom graph skills into iGCSE-style algebraic interpretation, coordinate reasoning, and direct equation-to-graph matching.

curriculumCoverage.year10CoverageNote: In Year 10 this topic supports advanced graph reading and line-equation fluency used in stronger end-of-year sets.
curriculumCoverage.year11CoverageNote: In Year 11 it becomes part of fuller iGCSE graph interpretation, transformation, and algebra linkage work.

visualSupport.generatedImagePrompt: An iGCSE Year 10 maths visual showing linear graphs, gradient triangles, y-intercepts, and equation matching with clean exam-style labels.
visualSupport.altText: Advanced Year 10 iGCSE linear graphs visual showing gradient and intercept links.

sections.title: Explain Simply
sections.body: A linear graph shows a constant rate of change, so its line stays straight and its gradient stays the same.

sections.title: Worked Examples
sections.body: Read the y-intercept first, then use two clear points to calculate the gradient before matching the equation.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
sections.body: Students often confuse the intercept with the gradient or read the scale incorrectly when points do not sit on every grid mark.

sections.title: Exam Technique
sections.body: Label coordinates cleanly and write the gradient method line by line so marks are protected even if the final equation slips.

quiz.prompt: A line crosses the y-axis at 3 and has gradient 2. Which equation is correct?
